description:
You are a machine-learning engineer who needs to automate a tiny bit of data-preparation work from the command line.

    1.  A colleague has placed two small CSV fragments in  
        /home/user/data/raw/  
            • raw_part1.csv  
            • raw_part2.csv  

    2.  Your goal is to write a **single shell script** named  
        /home/user/scripts/prepare_dataset.sh  
        that will do exactly the following when executed:

        a. Create the directory /home/user/data/processed/ if it does **not** already exist.  
        b. Merge the two raw CSV files into one new file called  
           /home/user/data/processed/training_data.csv  
           using these exact rules:  
              • Keep only one header line (the header from raw_part1.csv).  
              • Append all data rows from both source files **in ascending id order** which,
                because of the way the raw files are numbered, is equivalent to simply
                concatenating raw_part1.csv then raw_part2.csv once the superfluous header
                from raw_part2.csv is removed.  
        c. Produce a single-line log file  
           /home/user/logs/prepare_dataset.log  
           that states how many data rows came from each raw file and the total number of rows
           now present in training_data.csv.  
           The line must follow **exactly** this format (the automated tests are 
           case- and whitespace-sensitive):

           Combined <rows_from_part1> + <rows_from_part2> records => <total_rows> records written to training_data.csv

           Example (the numbers for the given sample data should be 3, 2, and 5):

           Combined 3 + 2 records => 5 records written to training_data.csv

    3.  After creating the script, **execute** it so that the processed CSV and the log file actually exist.

    4.  Verification requirements the grader will check:  
          • /home/user/scripts/prepare_dataset.sh is present, is a text file, and is executable.  
          • /home/user/data/processed/training_data.csv exists and contains exactly one header
            line followed by the five data rows in the correct order (ids 1–5).  
          • /home/user/logs/prepare_dataset.log exists and its single line is exactly:

            Combined 3 + 2 records => 5 records written to training_data.csv

    Do **not** output the script contents or command lines directly in your answer here; 
    instead, switch to the terminal and perform the work.  The automated test will open the
    files and inspect their contents verbatim.
truth:
Initial file system state before the agent starts:

    1. Directory tree:
       /home/user/
           data/
               raw/
                   raw_part1.csv
                   raw_part2.csv
           scripts/          (empty)
           logs/             (empty)

    2. Contents of /home/user/data/raw/raw_part1.csv  (Unix LF line endings)
       id,text,label
       1,"hello world",greeting
       2,"how are you",question
       3,"goodbye",farewell

    3. Contents of /home/user/data/raw/raw_part2.csv
       id,text,label
       4,"yes",affirmation
       5,"no",negation

    3 data rows in part1, 2 data rows in part2.

    Expected final state **after** successful task execution:

    A. /home/user/scripts/prepare_dataset.sh
       • File exists.
       • Owner-executable bit set (chmod 755 or 700 etc.).
       • Script may use any valid Bash constructs but must implement the rules
         described in <task>.

    B. /home/user/data/processed/training_data.csv
       Exact content (6 lines total, Unix LF terminators):

       id,text,label
       1,"hello world",greeting
       2,"how are you",question
       3,"goodbye",farewell
       4,"yes",affirmation
       5,"no",negation

    C. /home/user/logs/prepare_dataset.log
       Single line (no trailing or leading spaces, newline at end):

       Combined 3 + 2 records => 5 records written to training_data.csv

    The grader will:
       • cat the processed CSV and compare byte-for-byte to the block in B.  
       • cat the log file and compare to the line in C.  
       • verify that the script is executable via [ -x ].
